title: The Crosswalk
text:
The Crosswalk is the thinking layer of Lunaland.
These short field notes follow how data moves from observation to interpretation — and where uncertainty enters along the way.
Projects show what I build.
The Pitfalls Lab shows where things break.
The Concept Atlas shows how ideas connect.
The Crosswalk shows how I think through it.

title: Lunaland
text:
Lunaland is a portfolio ecosystem about turning complex biological data into interpretable, usable insight.
It connects workflow development, statistical interpretation, bioinformatics judgment and science communication into one coherent story.

title: Lunaland
text:
The organizing idea
Biological data rarely moves cleanly from experiment to conclusion. It passes through quality control, assumptions, metadata, uncertainty, statistical modeling and communication.
This site documents that path.

title: Projects
text: Projects are the build layer of Lunaland: reproducible workflows, statistical explainers and interactive tools.

title: Batch Effects
text:
Treating technical structure in the data as biological signal.
The classic version: samples cluster cleanly, groups separate nicely, and the result looks convincing.
But the separation is driven by when, where or how the samples were processed, not by biology.

title: Batch Effects
text:
Treating technical structure in the data as biological signal.
The classic version: samples cluster cleanly, groups separate nicely, and the result looks convincing.
But the separation is driven by when, where or how the samples were processed, not by biology.

title: Batch Effects
text:
Why it happens
Most datasets are not generated under perfectly controlled conditions.
Common sources:

samples processed on different days
sequencing runs split across lanes
library prep done in batches
different technicians or centers
case and control groups handled at different times

The critical failure is not the presence of batch effects.
It is when batch and biological condition overlap.
At that point, the data cannot tell you which is which.

title: Batch Effects
text:
What breaks
Batch effects don’t just add noise. They add structure.
That structure can:

create artificial separation between groups
inflate differential expression results
hide real biological signal
make models look more confident than they should be

The dangerous part is that everything can look “clean”:

PCA plots show tight clusters

volcano plots show strong hits

models converge without warnings

But the interpretation is wrong.

title: Batch Effects
text:
How to detect it
This usually shows up during exploratory analysis.
Things that have saved me more than once:

PCA colored by both condition and batch
checking whether samples cluster by sequencing date instead of phenotype
looking at metadata early, not after modeling
counting how many batches exist per group

A simple but critical question:

If I remove the condition labels, would I still see the same clusters?

If yes, something else is driving the structure.

title: Batch Effects
text:
How to prevent it
Some problems are fixable. Some are not.
Good habits:

record batch variables in metadata (date, lane, center, prep)
balance cases and controls across batches when possible
check for confounding before modeling
include batch terms in the model only when design allows it
document limitations when batch and condition are inseparable

The uncomfortable truth:

If batch and condition are perfectly confounded, no statistical method can fully recover the biology.

At that point, interpretation needs to be cautious, not clever.

title: Batch Effects
text:
Tiny demo
A minimal example of how misleading structure can look:



sample
condition
batch

case
batch1

control

control

If you color by condition:

Looks biological.
If you color by batch:

The plot did not lie.
It just answered a different question.

title: Batch Effects
text:
Suggested figure
Two PCA plots side by side:

left: colored by condition → clean separation

right: colored by batch → same separation

Caption:

The same structure can support two different stories depending on what you color by.

title: Wrong Genome Build
text:
A workflow uses genomic coordinates, variants or annotations from one genome build while interpreting them against another.
For example, a file may contain coordinates based on GRCh37/hg19, but the analysis assumes GRCh38/hg38.
This can look harmless because the chromosome names and positions still appear valid. The problem is that the same coordinate can refer to a different genomic context depending on the reference build.

title: Wrong Genome Build
text:
A workflow uses genomic coordinates, variants or annotations from one genome build while interpreting them against another.
For example, a file may contain coordinates based on GRCh37/hg19, but the analysis assumes GRCh38/hg38.
This can look harmless because the chromosome names and positions still appear valid. The problem is that the same coordinate can refer to a different genomic context depending on the reference build.

title: Wrong Genome Build
text:
Why it happens
Genome build mismatches often happen when data are combined from multiple sources.
Common causes include:

downloading public data without checking the reference build
mixing older variant resources with newer annotations
reusing BED, GTF or VCF files from a previous project
assuming chr1 versus 1 is the main compatibility issue
losing reference build information during file transfer or preprocessing

The mistake is easy to miss because many tools will still run.

title: Wrong Genome Build
text:
What breaks
A wrong genome build can affect both technical outputs and biological interpretation.
It can lead to:

variants mapped to the wrong gene or regulatory region
incorrect overlap with promoters, enhancers or exons
missing or false-positive intersections in BEDTools-style analyses
misleading annotation of genomic bins
incorrect gene-level summaries
biological claims based on shifted genomic context

The most dangerous part is that the output may look clean. A table can have coordinates, gene names and p-values while still being built on the wrong reference assumption.

title: Wrong Genome Build
text:
How to detect it
Useful checks include:

confirm the reference build from the original data source
inspect file headers, especially VCF headers
check whether chromosome naming conventions match expected files
compare a few known loci against a genome browser
examine whether many overlaps unexpectedly disappear
check whether coordinates exceed chromosome lengths for the assumed build
document the genome build explicitly in the project README or config file

A small sanity check is often more useful than trusting that the pipeline completed.

title: Wrong Genome Build
text:
How to prevent it
Preventing this problem requires making the reference build a visible part of the workflow.
Good habits include:

store genome build information in params.yaml or another config file
keep genome FASTA, annotation and index files in clearly named folders
avoid generic names like genome.fa or annotation.gtf
include build names in file paths, for example GRCh38/
validate that FASTA, GTF, BED and VCF files belong to the same build
record reference sources and versions in documentation
avoid mixing downloaded resources unless their build compatibility is confirmed

In a reproducible workflow, the genome build should not live in someone’s memory. It should live in the project structure.

title: Wrong Genome Build
text:
Tiny demo
Imagine we are checking whether variants overlap a gene region.
```r library(tibble)
variants <- tibble::tribble( ~variant_id, ~chromosome, ~position, ~assumed_build, “var_001”, “chr1”, 1000000, “GRCh38”, “var_002”, “chr1”, 2000000, “GRCh38”, “var_003”, “chr1”, 3000000, “GRCh37” )
expected_build <- “GRCh38”
variants |> dplyr::mutate( build_check = ifelse( assumed_build == expected_build, “OK”, “Check build before interpretation” ) )

title: E3 Atlas
text:
The E3 Atlas is a small concept map for the portfolio. It connects the ideas that appear across projects, Shiny apps, Crosswalk posts and the Bioinformatics Pitfalls Lab.
Projects show what I can build.
Field Notes show how I think.
The Pitfalls Lab shows my judgment.
The Atlas shows how the ideas connect.

title: E3 Atlas
text:
How to read the Atlas
Each concept has two definitions:

a plain-language definition for quick understanding
a technical definition for more precise interpretation

The links are intentionally lightweight. A concept can point to related projects, Crosswalk posts or Pitfalls Lab modules without needing a separate page yet.

title: E3 Atlas
text:
Main concept groups

Statistics and interpretation
These concepts support the genetic association Shiny app and Crosswalk posts about uncertainty, evidence and communication.
Examples include odds ratio, confidence interval, statistical significance, effect size, sample size and uncertainty.


Bioinformatics and RNA-seq
These concepts support the RNA-seq pipeline demo and explain how raw sequencing data becomes structured output.
Examples include quality control, read trimming, alignment, quantification, normalization and differential expression.


Pitfalls and data integrity
These concepts connect directly to the Bioinformatics Pitfalls Lab.
Examples include genome build, annotation file, gene ID, batch effect, metadata, low counts, multiple testing and overinterpretation.

title: E3 Atlas
text:
Future upgrades
Version 1 is intentionally simple. Later, this Atlas could grow into:

a searchable concept table
tag filters by topic or project
individual concept pages
a network map showing connections between concepts
backlinks from projects, Crosswalk posts and Pitfalls Lab modules

title: About
text:
I am interested in how scientific knowledge is built, challenged, and sometimes misunderstood. My work sits at the intersection of biology, statistics, and computation, but my curiosity extends beyond any single field.
The same principles that shape genomics also shape physics, economics, and even theology: we observe, we model, we interpret, and we make decisions under uncertainty.
History shows us that some of the most powerful ideas come from crossing boundaries. Einstein relied deeply on mathematics at a time when physics resisted it. Alan Turing, a mathematician, proposed a mechanism for biological pattern formation that was ignored for decades before becoming foundational.
These stories are not exceptions — they are reminders that knowledge advances when we allow ideas to move freely between disciplines.
At the same time, science is a human process. It carries bias, incentives, and limitations. Data can mislead, pipelines can break, and results can be overinterpreted.
My work focuses on making those weak points visible — and turning them into opportunities for better reasoning, better tools, and clearer communication.



GitHub
LinkedIn

title: Bioinformatics Pitfalls Lab
text:
The Bioinformatics Pitfalls Lab is a collection of short practical modules about common mistakes that can quietly distort biological interpretation.
These are not textbook chapters. They are debugging notes from the messy middle of analysis, where files, assumptions and metadata have to line up before results can be trusted.

title: From genomic data to statistical interpretation
text:
This project was built as a compact portfolio demonstration of an idea that sits at the center of modern bioinformatics and biostatistics: data alone are not enough. To generate meaningful insight, we need both a reliable computational workflow to process raw data and a statistical framework to evaluate what the results actually mean.
This page brings together two connected components:

A lightweight RNA-seq workflow demo showing how raw sequencing reads move through quality control, processing, and quantification

An interactive Shiny app showing how statistical evidence is interpreted through effect sizes, confidence intervals, and sample size

Together, they illustrate a full analytical story: from data generation to interpretation.

title: From genomic data to statistical interpretation
text:
This project was built as a compact portfolio demonstration of an idea that sits at the center of modern bioinformatics and biostatistics: data alone are not enough. To generate meaningful insight, we need both a reliable computational workflow to process raw data and a statistical framework to evaluate what the results actually mean.
This page brings together two connected components:

A lightweight RNA-seq workflow demo showing how raw sequencing reads move through quality control, processing, and quantification

An interactive Shiny app showing how statistical evidence is interpreted through effect sizes, confidence intervals, and sample size

Together, they illustrate a full analytical story: from data generation to interpretation.

title: From genomic data to statistical interpretation
text:
1. Problem
Genomic datasets are complex, high dimensional, and sensitive to processing choices. Before any biological or clinical conclusion can be made, raw data must first be transformed into structured, analyzable outputs through a reproducible pipeline. Even then, the work is not finished. Statistical analysis is needed to evaluate whether an observed pattern is meaningful, how uncertain it is, and how strongly it should inform a decision.
This matters because strong-looking results are not always reliable, and statistically significant results are not always important in practice. In both research and applied genomics settings, good analysis depends on two things working together:

a transparent and reproducible data-processing workflow
a clear statistical interpretation framework

This portfolio project was designed to show both.

title: From genomic data to statistical interpretation
text:
2. Workflow overview
At a high level, the analytical process looks like this:
Raw sequencing data → quality control → read processing → alignment / quantification → structured outputs → statistical analysis → interpretation



Workflow diagram


The first half of this process focuses on computational reproducibility: making sure that data are processed consistently, with clear inputs, outputs, and assumptions.
The second half focuses on statistical reasoning: understanding effect size, uncertainty, and the practical meaning of the result.

title: From genomic data to statistical interpretation
text:
3. RNA-seq pipeline section

Purpose
The RNA-seq component demonstrates how raw sequencing reads can be organized into a lightweight but realistic workflow for differential expression analysis. The goal was not to build a production-scale pipeline, but to show sound workflow design, reproducibility, and clear data flow.


Included steps
The pipeline structure includes the core stages commonly used in RNA-seq analysis:

Quality control with FastQC
Read trimming
Alignment with STAR
Counting with featureCounts
Differential expression analysis with DESeq2



Why this matters
In bioinformatics work, the value of a pipeline is not only in whether it runs, but in whether it is understandable, reproducible, and easy to extend. For that reason, this demo emphasizes:

modular workflow structure
explicit inputs and outputs
parameterization
environment specification
realistic organization of files and steps

These choices make it easier to inspect how data move through the system and how results were generated.


Reproducibility signals
The workflow is intentionally structured to highlight reproducibility practices, including:

a Nextflow-style organization
a central workflow file
configurable parameters
environment definition
documented execution steps
clear folder structure for inputs, outputs, and intermediate results



Example output



PCA plot





Volcano plot




Repository
The code for the RNA-seq demo is available here:
View the RNA-seq workflow repository

title: From genomic data to statistical interpretation
text:
4. Statistical analysis section

Purpose
Once processed data have been transformed into analyzable outputs, the next challenge is interpretation. The statistical component of this portfolio focuses on a simple but important question:
When does an observed association represent meaningful evidence?
To explore that question, I built an interactive Shiny app around a case-control genetics scenario. The app allows users to vary the number of cases, controls, and variant carriers, and then observe how the estimated association changes.


Core concepts
The app focuses on a small number of high-value concepts:

odds ratios as measures of association
confidence intervals as measures of uncertainty
the role of sample size in shaping precision
the difference between apparent signal and interpretable evidence

This section was designed to make statistical reasoning more visible and accessible rather than treating model output as self-explanatory.


Why this matters
In genomics and health data analysis, interpretation often fails when numerical results are presented without context. A point estimate by itself can be misleading. A statistically significant result may still be unstable, small in magnitude, or difficult to interpret in practice. Conversely, a potentially important effect may appear inconclusive if the sample size is too limited.
This is why I wanted the statistical component of the portfolio to center not only on calculation, but on interpretation.


App preview



Shiny app screenshot




Live app / repository
Open the Shiny app
View the app source code

title: From genomic data to statistical interpretation
text:
5. Interpretation section
This project is ultimately about how analytical outputs become useful evidence.
A well-structured pipeline can produce clean counts, summary tables, and differential expression results. A statistical app can calculate odds ratios and confidence intervals. But the key step is understanding what those outputs support and what they do not.
There are three ideas I wanted to make especially clear in this portfolio:

Effect size matters
A result is more informative when we understand the magnitude of the observed effect, not only whether it crosses a significance threshold. In practice, the size of an effect often matters more than the binary label of “significant” or “not significant.”


Uncertainty matters
Confidence intervals provide context that a single estimate cannot. They show how precise the estimate is and whether multiple plausible interpretations remain compatible with the data.


Context matters
Results do not exist in isolation. Their meaning depends on study design, sample size, data quality, assumptions, and the specific question being asked. Analytical outputs should support judgment, not replace it.
In research and clinical-adjacent settings, this kind of interpretation is essential. Decisions are rarely based on one number alone. They rely on a combination of workflow quality, statistical evidence, and domain context.

title: From genomic data to statistical interpretation
text:
6. Data and context
This portfolio project uses public, example, or simulated data for demonstration purposes.
The goal is not to reproduce a specific biological finding or clinical result, but to illustrate the structure of an end-to-end analytical workflow and the reasoning used to interpret outputs responsibly.
Although simplified, the components reflect the kinds of practices used in research and applied genomics settings:

transforming raw data into structured outputs
maintaining reproducibility and transparency
evaluating statistical evidence with attention to uncertainty
presenting results in a way that supports interpretation

This makes the project suitable as a compact demonstration of workflow thinking rather than as a production or diagnostic system.

title: From genomic data to statistical interpretation
text:
7. Design decisions
This site was intentionally designed to be minimal.

Why only one main project page?
Because the strongest version of this portfolio is not a list of disconnected tools. It is a single analytical narrative. The RNA-seq workflow and the Shiny app are presented together because they address different parts of the same problem.


Why keep the examples lightweight?
Given the time constraint, the priority was clarity over scale. A smaller, well-explained project is more convincing than a larger project with weak framing or incomplete documentation.


Why emphasize interpretation?
Many technical portfolios stop at computation. I wanted this one to show the next step: how outputs are evaluated, communicated, and connected back to the original question.


Trade-offs and assumptions
This project makes several simplifying choices:

the RNA-seq workflow is a compact demonstration rather than a production pipeline
the statistical app focuses on one concept deeply rather than covering many methods superficially
examples are chosen for interpretability and communication value
public or simulated data are used to avoid privacy concerns and keep the project shareable

These trade-offs were deliberate. The goal was to build a portfolio piece that is realistic, focused, and easy to understand.

title: From genomic data to statistical interpretation
text:
What this project demonstrates
Across both components, this portfolio highlights the following strengths:

workflow design and reproducibility
clear input-to-output reasoning
statistical interpretation beyond surface-level computation
communication across technical and non-technical audiences
practical awareness of assumptions and limitations

title: From genomic data to statistical interpretation
text:
Next steps
Potential future extensions include:

adding a more detailed workflow diagram
expanding the RNA-seq demo with richer summary outputs
integrating example downstream plots from differential expression analysis
adding a short methods appendix for technically interested readers

For now, the site is intentionally scoped to present one clear idea well: how raw genomic data become interpretable statistical evidence.

title: RNA-seq Workflow Demo: From Raw Reads to Interpretation
text:
Scientific data is messy.
Before we interpret results, data moves through multiple steps — each one making assumptions, introducing structure, and sometimes creating unintended patterns.
This project shows how RNA-seq data moves through that process, and where interpretation can become uncertain.

title: RNA-seq Workflow Demo: From Raw Reads to Interpretation
text:
Scientific data is messy.
Before we interpret results, data moves through multiple steps — each one making assumptions, introducing structure, and sometimes creating unintended patterns.
This project shows how RNA-seq data moves through that process, and where interpretation can become uncertain.

title: RNA-seq Workflow Demo: From Raw Reads to Interpretation
text:
The Question
Why does analyzing RNA-seq data require more than just running a pipeline?

title: RNA-seq Workflow Demo: From Raw Reads to Interpretation
text:
Workflow Overview
RNA-seq analysis is often presented as a sequence of steps:
Raw data → quality control → processing → statistical analysis → interpretation
In practice, each of these steps transforms the data.

Quality control decides what data is considered usable

Alignment maps reads to a reference that may not perfectly represent the sample

Quantification reduces complex read data into gene-level summaries

Statistical analysis highlights patterns under specific assumptions

At each step, information is filtered, reshaped, and interpreted.
The final results depend not only on the data itself, but on how these transformations are applied.

title: RNA-seq Workflow Demo: From Raw Reads to Interpretation
text:
What I Built
This project implements a small, modular RNA-seq workflow designed to make each step visible and reproducible.
It includes:

Structured workflow steps (QC, alignment, quantification, analysis)

A differential expression analysis using gene-level counts

Clear input and output organization

Explicit configuration for reproducibility

The goal is not to present a production-ready pipeline.
The goal is to show how analysis decisions are structured, and how they affect interpretation.

title: RNA-seq Workflow Demo: From Raw Reads to Interpretation
text:
Outputs

PCA plot



PCA plot showing sample clustering


A PCA plot summarizes variation across samples.
It can reveal clustering patterns, potential batch effects, or outliers.
It does not explain why samples cluster — only that they do.



Volcano plot



Volcano plot showing differential expression


A volcano plot highlights genes with large changes and strong statistical signals.
It helps prioritize candidates for further investigation.
It does not confirm biological relevance.



Results table (snippet)



Gene
log2 Fold Change
p-value
Adjusted p-value




GeneA
1.8
0.001
0.01


GeneB
-2.1
0.002
0.02


GeneC
0.5
0.05
0.10



This table summarizes statistical outputs.
It shows which genes differ between conditions under a specific model.
It does not explain the biological mechanism behind those differences.

title: RNA-seq Workflow Demo: From Raw Reads to Interpretation
text:
Interpretation
Differential expression analysis identifies genes whose expression levels differ between conditions.
This is a statistical statement, not a biological conclusion.
A gene can appear statistically significant because:

the effect size is large

the variability is low

the sample size is sufficient

But statistical significance does not guarantee biological importance.
Effect size, variability, and context all matter.
Patterns in data do not automatically translate into biological insight — they require interpretation.

title: RNA-seq Workflow Demo: From Raw Reads to Interpretation
text:
Where Things Go Wrong
Some errors are invisible at first glance.
A Wrong Genome Build can shift coordinates so that reads map to the wrong locations, producing results that look valid but refer to the wrong biology.
Batch Effects can introduce systematic differences between groups that are unrelated to the biological condition, creating patterns that appear meaningful but are driven by technical structure.

title: RNA-seq Workflow Demo: From Raw Reads to Interpretation
text:
Connecting to Statistical Reasoning
These challenges are not unique to RNA-seq.
They reflect broader questions about uncertainty, sample size, and interpretation.
See: When is a genetic association real?
Statistical results depend on how much data we have, how variable it is, and how we model it.
Understanding those limits is part of interpreting any result.

title: RNA-seq Workflow Demo: From Raw Reads to Interpretation
text:
Key Concepts

Batch effect — differences driven by experimental conditions rather than biology

Sample metadata — information about how samples were collected and processed

PCA — a way to summarize variation across samples

Differential expression — statistical comparison of gene expression between groups

Normalization — adjusting counts to make samples comparable

title: RNA-seq Workflow Demo: From Raw Reads to Interpretation
text:
Limitations
This workflow is designed as a demonstration.

It uses public or simplified data

It is not a clinical-grade pipeline

Some steps are intentionally simplified to highlight structure rather than edge cases

The focus is on interpretation, not optimization.

title: RNA-seq Workflow Demo: From Raw Reads to Interpretation
text:
Closing
Understanding data is not just about building pipelines — it is about knowing where results come from, what assumptions they carry, and how easily they can mislead.

title: When is a genetic association real?
text: If a genetic variant appears more often in cases than controls, when can we say the association is real?

title: When is a genetic association real?
text: If a genetic variant appears more often in cases than controls, when can we say the association is real?

title: When is a genetic association real?
text:
2. The concept
Genetic association studies often summarize results using an odds ratio (OR).

OR > 1 → variant is more common in cases

OR < 1 → variant is less common in cases

But an odds ratio on its own is not enough.
Every estimate comes with uncertainty, typically expressed as a confidence interval (CI).
This interval reflects how much the estimate could reasonably change if we observed new data.
Interpretation lives in the relationship between:

the effect size (OR)

the uncertainty (CI)

the amount of data supporting both

title: When is a genetic association real?
text:
3. A simple example
Imagine a small study:

100 cases

100 controls

Variant carriers:

Cases: 20

Controls: 10

This produces an odds ratio greater than 1, suggesting a possible association.
Now imagine repeating the same calculation with:

a much larger dataset

or slightly different counts

The odds ratio might stay similar, but the confidence interval will shrink or expand depending on how stable the data is.
Same number, different meaning.

title: When is a genetic association real?
text:
4. What can go wrong
This is where interpretation often breaks:

Focusing on the odds ratio alone
Treating OR = 2 as strong evidence without asking how stable that estimate is
Ignoring confidence intervals
A wide interval may include 1, meaning the data is compatible with no association
Equating statistical significance with importance
Large datasets can produce very small, statistically significant effects that are not meaningful
Underestimating sample size effects
Small datasets produce unstable estimates that can shift dramatically

title: When is a genetic association real?
text:
5. Why this matters across fields
This pattern shows up everywhere.

In genomics → variant associations depend on sample size and cohort structure

In RNA-seq → differential expression depends on count variability and replication

In clinical research → treatment effects depend on both magnitude and uncertainty

In machine learning → performance estimates depend on data splits and variability

Across fields, the same idea holds:

An estimate without uncertainty is incomplete.
An estimate without enough data is unstable.

title: When is a genetic association real?
text:
6. Takeaway
A genetic association is not defined by a single number.
It requires:

an effect size (odds ratio)

an estimate of uncertainty (confidence interval)

enough data to make that estimate stable

Interpretation is not about whether a result exists.
It is about how much we can trust it.

title: When is a genetic association real?
text:
7. Related

Interactive app: When is a genetic association real?
(Shiny app – coming soon)
Project: /projects/rnaseq-workflow.qmd
→ See how variability and sample size affect downstream interpretation
